does: This paper surveys the field of machine unlearning, providing a comprehensive taxonomy of techniques and approaches for removing or modifying learned information in trained machine learning models. It categorizes existing methods into data deletion, perturbation, and model update strategies, and documents commonly used evaluation metrics and benchmark datasets. The survey synthesizes the current state-of-the-art in unlearning, offering systematic coverage of how practitioners and researchers approach the challenge of selectively forgetting information while preserving model utility, alongside identification of key performance bottlenecks and failure modes in existing approaches.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in machine learning privacy and model modification, extending earlier work on machine forgetting and data deletion from models. It synthesizes connections between the privacy-preserving learning literature (differential privacy, federated learning), adversarial robustness research that examines model vulnerabilities, and fairness work addressing bias removal. The survey bridges disparate methodological threads including gradient-based unlearning approaches, approximate influence functions, and catastrophic forgetting mitigation from continual learning, while responding to emerging regulatory requirements around the right to be forgotten and data governance.
enables: By systematizing the unlearning landscape, this survey enables development of more standardized evaluation frameworks and benchmarks for unlearning techniques, facilitating fair comparison across methods. It opens pathways toward more robust unlearning procedures that maintain transferability across model architectures and training regimes, and points toward interpretable unlearning mechanisms that clarify what information has been removed. The work supports practical deployment of adaptive ML systems that respond to privacy violations, fairness concerns, or security threats by efficiently removing compromised or problematic learned patterns without full retraining, while also motivating new theoretical foundations for understanding the limits and possibilities of selective information removal.
